text: Epictetus, who absorbed Chrysippan theory through the Roman Stoic tradition, translated the dry mechanics of impressions and assent into a practice he called prosoche — attention to oneself. He advised students to treat every incoming impression as a visitor at the gate: do not admit it until you have examined its credentials. The examination need not be long. A moment of deliberate pause — what later contemplatives would call 'the gap' — is sufficient to interrupt the automatic sequence from impression to impulse to action. Epictetus called this gap our one unconditional freedom: circumstances may force impressions upon us, but they cannot force assent.
question: What did Epictetus mean by treating an impression like 'a visitor at the gate'?

options.text: Welcoming strong impressions and rejecting weak ones based on their intensity
Delaying all decisions until the impression has been forgotten
Examining the impression's credentials before admitting it into assent
Categorizing impressions by their source as either internal or external
